titleStmt.title: Berry curvature derived negative magnetoconductivity observed in type-II magnetic Weyl semimetal films

affiliation.orgName: RIKEN Center for Emergent Matter Science (CEMS)
RIKEN Center for Emergent Matter Science (CEMS)

analytic.title: Berry curvature derived negative magnetoconductivity observed in type-II magnetic Weyl semimetal films

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Here we study nonmonotonic features which appear both in magnetoresistivity and anomalous Hall resistivity during the simple magnetization process, by systematically measuring type-II magnetic Weyl semimetal EuCd 2 Sb 2 films over a wide carrier density range. We find that a positive magnetoresistivity hump can be explained as manifestation of a field-linear term in the generalized magnetoconductivity formula including the Berry curvature. As also confirmed by model calculation, the term can be negative and pronounced near the Weyl point energy in the case that the Weyl cones are heavily tilted. Our findings demonstrate extensive effects of the Berry curvature on various magnetotransport in magnetic Weyl semimetals beyond the anomalous Hall effect.

Weyl semimetals (WSMs) host pairs of three-dimensional band crossing points called Weyl points in their bulk band structure, where the spin degeneracy is resolved by breaking of either space-inversion or time-reversal symmetry [1][2][3] . Magnetotransport reflecting the Berry curvature has recently been attracting great interest in condensed matter physics 4 .

Owing to the large Berry curvature distribution around the Weyl points, magnetic WSMs with time-reversal symmetry breaking exhibit giant magnetoelectric responses such as in anomalous Hall effect (AHE) [5][6][7] , anomalous Nernst effect (ANE) 8,9 , chiral anomaly [10][11][12][13] , and magneto-optical effect 14 . It has been also theoretically proposed that nontrivial coupling between charge and magnetization originating in the chiral anomaly enables further versatile functionalities such as charge-induced spin torque and electric field-induced magnetization switching 15,16 .

For magnetic WSMs, 3d-electron systems including Co 3 Sn 2 S 2 [17][18][19][20][21][22][23] , Co 2 MnGa 24-29 , and Mn 3 Sn [30][31][32][33][34] have been intensively studied so far. AHE and also ANE are largely enhanced due to the presence of multiple Weyl points in the ferromagnetic ground state and observed up to high temperatures near or well above room temperature 6,8,9,35 . However, their band structures with many Weyl and non-Weyl bands near the Fermi level are too complex to accurately evaluate magnetotransport derived from the Weyl points. On the other hand, 4f -electron magnetic WSMs including GdPtBi [36][37][38][39] , EuCd 2 Sb 2 [40][41][42][43][44] While magnetotransport of 4f -electron magnetic WSMs has been studied mainly focusing on the forced ferromagnetic state, intriguing features may appear also during the magnetization process. One typical example is the nonmonotonic behavior observed in the anomalous

Hall resistivity below B s 48,50-52 . Furthermore, it has been theoretically proposed that the Berry curvature can also affect magnetoconductivity or magnetoresistivity especially when the Weyl cones are tilted as in type-II WSMs 53 .

Here we systematically study magnetotransport of EuCd 2 Sb 2 thin films over a wide carrier density range, particularly focusing on nonmonotonic features appearing below the saturation field. In addition to the nonmonotonic feature in the anomalous Hall resistivity, we find that a broad hump appears in the magnetoresistivity curve. This can be interpreted as manifestation of a field-linear term in the generalized magnetoconductivity formula including the Berry curvature.

EuCd 2 Sb 2 single-crystalline films were grown on CdTe (111)A substrate using a molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) technique 48 . In order to remove native oxide and obtain an atomically smooth surface, CdTe substrates were etched with 0.01 % Br 2 -methanol and heated to 680 • C under Cd flux inside the MBE chamber prior to the growth 54,55 . EuCd 2 Sb 2 films were then grown at 324 • C by codeposition of Eu, Cd, and Sb with Cd rich pressure ratios (P Cd /(P Eu +P Sb ) ∼ 30 -60). For achieving high crystallinity, the films were annealed at 453

• C for 3 minutes with supplying excess Cd after the growth. Growth orientation is along the c-axis, and the film thickness was designed at 50 nm. Since Cd is easily deficient in EuCd 2 Sb 2 , we have obtained EuCd 2 Sb 2 films with hole densities ranging from 10 18 to 10 20 cm -3 . Longitudinal resistivity ρ xx and Hall resistivity ρ yx were measured with employing a standard four-probe method on a Hall bar. Aluminum wire was connected to the terminals by using an ultrasonic bonding machine and these connections were reinforced by appending silver paste. Low-temperature measurements of ρ xx and ρ yx up to 9 T were performed using a Cryomagnetics cryostat system equipped with a superconducting magnet.

Magnetization curves up to 7 T were measured using a superconducting quantum interfer-ence device magnetometer in a Quantum Design Magnetic Property Measurement System (MPMS). Theoretical analysis on the transport properties were conducted numerically using on-premise pc clusters. Strictly, in some samples such as the film with p 3D = 5.0 × 10 19 cm -3 , the hump structure in ρ AHE yx remains visible up to about 10 K. This also suggests that the nonmonotonic feature is derived from the momentum-space band Berry curvature, because in this case the finite change can be induced by the magnetic field even above T N . Another hump structure only seen in the magnetoresistivity near zero field is probably ascribed to a zero-field centered negative cusp structure caused by weak anti-localization effect 56,57 . This also indicates that the nonmonotonic feature is derived from the momentum-space band Berry curvature, not from the spin Berry phase reflecting the real-space magnetic ordering. The nonmonotonic feature of σ xx can be confirmed also in previous reports of other type-II magnetic WSMs 52,58 . However, there had been little discussion about its origins.

To discuss the Berry curvature effect on the magnetoconductivity, here we refer to a generalized formulation derived by solving the Boltzman equation with incorporating the Berry curvature 4 . According to this, the current density J B (1) and J B (2) , proportional to the first 53 and second 59 powers of the magnetic field, are expressed as
div.formula: J B(1) = e 3 τ n=± dp 3 (2π) 3 W pn (E • v pn )(f 0 pn ) ′ -e 3 τ n=± dp 3 (2π) 3 (B • E)(b pn • v pn )v pn (f 0 pn ) ′ ,(1)
J B(2) = -e 4 τ n dp 3 (2π) 3 [W pn (E • W pn )](f 0 pn ) ′ ,(2)
div.p: where (f 0 pn ) ′ is the energy derivative of the Fermi-Dirac distribution function at zero temperature with momentum p and band index n, and W pn represents b pn × (v pn × B) with the Berry curvature b pn and the velocity v pn . The term J B( 1) is finite only when the time-reversal symmetry is broken and the velocity is anisotropic with tilting of the Weyl cones, and so it is expected to be a leading term in the case of type-II WSMs 53 . The J B (2) term is always finite, giving general formula of the chiral anomaly. For the present case of E = (E x , 0, 0) and B = (0, 0, B z ), the magnetoconductivity σ
div.formula: xx = e 3 τ n dp 3 (2π) 3 v 2 x b z B z (f 0 pn ) ′ ,(3)
σ B(2) xx = -e 4 τ n dp 3 (2π) 3 v 2 x b 2 z B 2 z (f 0 pn ) ′ . (4
H = - k 2 2m + vk x σ x + vk y σ y + v 2 (k 2 z -k 2 0 )σ z .(6)

figure.head: as sketched in Fig. 1 (
figure.figDesc: a)43 . The Cd 5s and Sb 5p bands are inverted to form a simple band structure with type-II band crossings near the Fermi level, and the Eu 4f states provide large isotropic magnetic moments (Eu 2+ : S = 7/2, L = 0)40,41 . At zero magnetic field, inplane A-type antiferromagnetic ordering is realized below the Néel temperature T N = 7.5 K, where a gap opened by the antiferromagnetic ordering is negligibly small and the crossing points can be seen almost degenerate as Dirac points41 . By applying the magnetic field B along the c-axis, the magnetic moments are gradually canted without spin-flop transition and are fully aligned along the c-axis above the saturation field B s40 . During the simple magnetization process, a few pairs of Weyl points are formed by splitting of the Dirac points and shifted in energy and momentum with the Zeeman band splitting.

figure.head: Figure 1 (
figure.figDesc: Figure 1(b) presents magnetization curves taken for a EuCd 2 Sb 2 film with a hole density of p 3D = 0.53×10 19 cm -3 , measured with applying the magnetic field along the c-axis at various temperatures. At the base temperature of 2 K, the forced ferromagnetic state is realized above the saturation field B s = 3.5 T. Below B s , out-of-plane magnetization increases linearly upon increasing the field, confirming that there are no other magnetic transitions also in the thin film form. Figure 1(c) shows temperature dependence of resistivity taken for three EuCd 2 Sb 2 films with different hole densities of p 3D = 0.53, 5.0, and 11 × 10 19 cm -3 . All the films exhibit a kink structure at 7.5 K independent of the carrier density, consistent withT N previously reported for bulk single crystals40,41 .

figure.head: Figure 2
figure.figDesc: Figure 2 summarizes the magnetoresistivity ratio (ρ xx -ρ xx,0T )/ρ xx,0T and the anomalous Hall resistivity ρ AHE yx taken for these three EuCd 2 Sb 2 films under the out-of-plane field at various temperatures. ρ AHE yx is obtained by subtracting the ordinary Hall term from the raw ρ yx curve. No AHE appears at zero field, since the Berry curvature is zero in total in the antiferromagnetic ground state. When the magnetic field is applied along the c-axis direction, ρ AHE yx nonmonotonically increases and then decreases with a hump structure, and eventually saturates above B s . Detailed behavior is dependent on the hole density, while, again, no other magnetic transitions are confirmed. Therefore, this nonmonotonic dependence is interpreted as a change in the electronic structure and Berry curvature during the magnetization process, where the Weyl points are formed and shifted across the Fermi level with the Zeeman band splitting. This nonmonotonic dependence of ρ AHE yx with the energy shift of the Weyl points has been also theoretically demonstrated for antiferromagnetic EuTiO 3 , in which the Weyl points are similarly formed and shifted in the Zeeman splitting process 51 .As confirmed in the upper panel of Fig.2(a), nonmonotonic behavior or a broad hump structure emerges also in the magnetoresistivity below B s . The field where the hump appears in (ρ xx -ρ xx,0T )/ρ xx,0T is slightly lower than that at which the hump appears in ρ AHE yx . On the other hand, both the humps similarly shift to lower fields upon increasing temperature and almost disappear above T N = 7.5 K. As seen in Figs.2(b) and 2(c), the same trend is commonly observed also in the other two films with higher hole densities. This suggests that the hump in (ρ xx -ρ xx,0T )/ρ xx,0T also reflects a field-induced change in the Berry curvature.

figure.head: Figure 3
figure.figDesc: Figure 3 shows hole-density dependence of (ρ xx -ρ xx,0T )/ρ xx,0T , ρ AHE yx , converted magnetoconductivity σ xx , and nonmonotonic anomalous Hall conductivity ∆σ AHE xy . As shown in Figs. 3(a) and 3(b), both (ρ xx -ρ xx,0T )/ρ xx,0T and ρ AHE yx humps are systematically observed in all the films and gradually shift to lower fields with increase in the hole density. These trends also suggest that both of them originate in the change in the Berry curvature. When converted to σ xx and anomalous Hall conductivity σ AHE xy , σ xx shows a negative hump at the original hump position in ρ xx , while σ AHE xy still shows a positive hump. For the high hole density samples, a negative hump also appears at lower fields in the nonmonotonic component of the anomalous Hall conductivity ∆σ AHE xy , as shown in Fig.3(d). This also indicates that the nonmonotonic feature is derived from the momentum-space band Berry curvature, not

xx can be positive or negative, depending on the sign of b z relative to B z , namely the splitting direction of the paired Weyl points and also the tilting direction of the Weyl cones. Since the sign of b z changes upon reversal of B z , σ B(1) xx is also even for the reversal of B z . Also note that the anomalous Hall conductivity xy , b z contributes as a linear effect. While b z is integrated only at E F in the equation of σ B(1) xx , it is integrated fully below E F in σ AHE xy . We further examine the relation between σ B(1) xx and σ AHE xy by calculating their Fermi energy dependence. We consider a dipolar model for type-II WSM, whose Hamiltonian reads

figure.head: Figure 4
figure.figDesc: Figure 4 shows the results for magnetoconductivity and anomalous Hall conductivity. They are nonzero below the chemical potential of µ ∼ 0.14 above which the hole bands are fully filled. σ B(1) xx exhibits a sharp negative peak almost at the Weyl point energy. On the
